nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in tide pools, lagoons, and outer reef slopes at depths from about 5 to 56 m, typically sheltering under rocky overhangs, in caves, and around branching corals. Adults have been reported either solitary, in pairs, or in small aggregations near the substrate.

diet.notes: Has been reported to specialize on small live zooplankton — live copepods (Tisbe, Tigriopus), amphipods, vitamin-enriched live brine shrimp nauplii, and small mysis. Wild-caught specimens have been reported to often refuse prepared and frozen foods initially, with frequent starvation risk; aquacultured specimens tend to be hardier and may eventually accept enriched frozen mysis, Cyclop-eeze, and Nutramar Ova. Generally recommended to feed small portions several times per day and to maintain a robust copepod population in the display or a connected refugium.

summary: A strikingly banded Indo-Pacific reef pipefish with red-and-white rings; generally considered expert-only due to specialized live-food needs and intolerance of competitive tankmates.
careNotes:
The Banded Pipefish (Dunckerocampus dactyliophorus), also widely known as the Ringed Pipefish, is a slow, deliberate swimmer that tends to shelter under overhangs, in caves, and among branching corals or gorgonians. Best kept as a mated pair or small group in a dedicated, mature reef aquarium with stable parameters, gentle flow, and a strong resident copepod/amphipod population. Wild-collected specimens have a poor survival record in captivity and are commonly considered expert-only; aquacultured specimens have been reported to be substantially hardier and easier to feed and are generally recommended where available. Generally not suited to mixed community reefs — fast or aggressive tankmates tend to outcompete pipefish for food, and anemones, large-polyp corals, large shrimp, and crabs have been reported to harm or prey on them. Tank-size sources disagree widely (LiveAquaria 50 US gal / ~190 L, ReefApp 100 L, Reeflex 250 L); a 200 L footprint with at least ~90 cm of length is used here, biased above the retailer minimum given the species' ~19 cm adult length, slow feeding, and need for swimming space and dedicated husbandry. Temperature sources also disagree: LiveAquaria gives 22.2–25.6°C while FishBase environmental data and Reeflex give ~24.8–29°C; an averaged 23–27°C range is used. LiveAquaria tags the species as 'venomous,' but FishBase, Reeflex ('toxic hazard unknown'), ReefApp, and reviewed Syngnathidae literature do not document venomous structures in Dunckerocampus dactyliophorus; venomousSpines is set to false pending evidence to the contrary. Reeflex notes the species has been reported to harass pregnant ornamental shrimp and eat eggs from their lamellae, so reefSafe is recorded as 'with caution.' Pipefish are documented to be sensitive to copper-based medications, so copperSensitive is set to true.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Temperature range refined from 23–27°C to 24–27°C. Re-verification of LiveAquaria (72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C) and FishBase (24.8–29°C from 672 occurrence records, mean 27.8°C) confirmed the two sources overlap only at ~24.8–25.6°C. A floor of 24°C (rounded down from FishBase's 24.8°C minimum) is defensible as the practical aquarium lower bound — it respects that this is a tropical species with no credible record of thriving below ~24°C, while still providing a small buffer below the FishBase floor. The ceiling of 27°C is retained as a safe aquarium target well below the wild maximum of 29°C. The prior 23°C floor was too low: it sat below the FishBase minimum and had no corroborating source. Wikipedia and ReefApp did not provide independent temperature data. The venomous claim from LiveAquaria remains uncorroborated by FishBase, Wikipedia, ReefApp, or Reeflex; venomousSpines=false is retained.
breedingNotes: Reproduction is ovoviviparous. After a courtship display, the female deposits adhesive eggs onto a flat brooding area on the underside of the male's trunk/tail, where the male oxygenates and broods them until release. Pairs are typically monogamous and have been reported to spawn at roughly 12-day intervals when well fed. Captive breeding has been reported and aquacultured juveniles are commercially available; rearing fry is generally considered advanced and requires a sustained supply of very small live zooplankton.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page reached via the pipefish collection root. Provided care-level, water-parameter, tank-size, temperament, and reef-compatibility figures. Tags species as 'venomous'; this is not corroborated by other sources and is treated with skepticism.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Syngnathidae, Syngnathiformes), max size 19 cm TL, depth range 5–56 m, Indo-Pacific range (Red Sea and East Africa to Samoa, north to Japan, south to Australia), environmental temperature 24.8–29°C (mean 27.8°C), and ovoviviparous reproduction. IUCN status Data Deficient.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Assigned first secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. Fell back through the slice's secondary list to Reeflex and ReefApp.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size 19 cm, temperature 24.8–29°C, 250 L minimum recommendation (used as high-end anchor in the tank-size disagreement), 'only for advanced aquarists' difficulty, live-food bias, and the note that the species has been reported to harass pregnant ornamental shrimp.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size 19 cm, ~26°C target, SG ~1.026, lifespan 2–3 years, peaceful temperament, and need for low flow plus solid structures for the tail to grip. Listed minimum tank as 100 L — used as low-end anchor in the tank-size disagreement.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive pairs have been reported to spawn in mature reef aquariums with stable parameters and abundant live food, at roughly 12-day intervals; raising fry is generally considered advanced and requires a continuous supply of very small live zooplankton.
